title: Removable discontinuities
description: Remove discontinuity points of piecewise functions by assigning appropriate values.

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $f(x)=\begin{cases}
\dfrac{x^2-4}{x+2}&\text{for }x\neq -2
\\\\
k&\text{for }x=-2
\end{cases}$

$f$ is continuous for all real numbers.

**What is the value of $k$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\dfrac{x^2-4}{x+2}$ is continuous for all real numbers other than $x=-2$ which means $f$ is continuous for all real numbers other than $x=-2$.

In order for $f$ to also be continuous at $x=-2$, the following equality must hold:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2}f(x)=f(-2)$

Since $f(-2)=k$, we will obtain the above equality by letting $k=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2}f(x)$.

So let's find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2}f(x)$, come on!

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2}f(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2}\dfrac{x^2-4}{x+2}\qquad\gray{\text{This is the rule for }x\neq-2}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2}\dfrac{\cancel{(x+2)}(x-2)}{\cancel{x+2}}\qquad\gray{\text{Factor}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2}(x-2)\qquad\gray{\text{Cancel common factors}}
\\\\
&\small\text{(This is allowed because }x\neq -2)
\\\\
&=(-2)-2\qquad\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=-4
\end{align}$

We obtained that if we set $k=-4$, then $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2}f(x)=f(-2)$, which makes $f$ continuous at $x=-2$.

Since we already saw that $f$ is continuous for any other real number, we can determine that it's continuous for all real numbers.
In conclusion, $k=-4$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $g(x)=\begin{cases}
\dfrac{x^2+5x+6}{x+3}&\text{for }x\neq -3
\\\\
k&\text{for }x=-3
\end{cases}$

$g$ is continuous for all real numbers.

**What is the value of $k$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\dfrac{x^2+5x+6}{x+3}$ is continuous for all real numbers other than $x=-3$ which means $g$ is continuous for all real numbers other than $x=-3$.

In order for $g$ to also be continuous at $x=-3$, the following equality must hold:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}g(x)=g(-3)$

Since $g(-3)=k$, we will obtain the above equality by letting $k=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}g(x)$.

So let's find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}g(x)$, come on!

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}g(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}\dfrac{x^2+5x+6}{x+3}\qquad\gray{\text{This is the rule for }x\neq -3}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}\dfrac{\cancel{(x+3)}(x+2)}{\cancel{(x+3)}}\qquad\gray{\text{Factor}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}{(x+2)}{}\qquad\gray{\text{Cancel common factors}}
\\\\
&\small\text{(This is allowed because }x\neq -3)
\\\\
&=(-3)+2\qquad\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=-1
\end{align}$

We obtained that if we set $k=-1$, then $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}g(x)=g(-3)$, which makes $g$ continuous at $x=-3$.

Since we already saw that $g$ is continuous for any other real number, we can determine that it's continuous for all real numbers.
In conclusion, $k=-1$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $h(x)=\begin{cases}
\dfrac{3x}{xe^{x}}&\text{for }x\neq 0
\\\\
k&\text{for }x=0
\end{cases}$

$h$ is continuous for all real numbers.

**What is the value of $k$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\dfrac{3x}{xe^{x}}$ is continuous for all real numbers other than $x=0$ which means $h$ is continuous for all real numbers other than $x=0$.

In order for $h$ to also be continuous at $x=0$, the following equality must hold:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}h(x)=h(0)$

Since $h(0)=k$, we will obtain the above equality by letting $k=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}h(x)$.

So let's find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}h(x)$, come on!

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}h(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\dfrac{3x}{xe^{x}}\qquad\gray{\text{This is the rule for }x\neq 0}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\dfrac{\cancel{3(x)}}{\cancel{(x)}(e^x)}\qquad\gray{\text{Factor}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\dfrac{3}{(e^x)}\qquad\gray{\text{Cancel common factors}}
\\\\
&\small\text{(This is allowed because }x\neq 0)
\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{e^{0}}\qquad\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=3
\end{align}$

We obtained that if we set $k=3$, then $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}h(x)=h(0)$, which makes $h$ continuous at $x=0$.

Since we already saw that $h$ is continuous for any other real number, we can determine that it's continuous for all real numbers.
In conclusion, $k=3$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $f(x)=\begin{cases}
\dfrac{15x}{x^3+3x}&\text{for }x\neq 0
\\\\
k&\text{for }x=0
\end{cases}$

$f$ is continuous for all real numbers.

**What is the value of $k$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\dfrac{15x}{x^3+3x}$ is continuous for all real numbers other than $x=0$ which means $f$ is continuous for all real numbers other than $x=0$.

In order for $f$ to also be continuous at $x=0$, the following equality must hold:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}f(x)=f(0)$

Since $f(0)=k$, we will obtain the above equality by letting $k=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}f(x)$.

So let's find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}f(x)$, come on!

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}f(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\dfrac{15x}{x^3+3x}\qquad\gray{\text{This is the rule for }x\neq 0}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\dfrac{15\cancel{(x)}}{\cancel{(x)}(x^2+3)}\qquad\gray{\text{Factor}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\dfrac{15}{(x^2+3)}\qquad\gray{\text{Cancel common factors}}
\\\\
&\small\text{(This is allowed because }x\neq 0)
\\\\
&=\dfrac{15}{(0)^2+3}\qquad\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=5
\end{align}$

We obtained that if we set $k=5$, then $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}f(x)=f(0)$, which makes $f$ continuous at $x=0$.

Since we already saw that $f$ is continuous for any other real number, we can determine that it's continuous for all real numbers.
In conclusion, $k=5$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $g(x)=\dfrac{x^2-x-12}{x-4}$ when $x\neq 4$.

$g$ is continuous for all real numbers.

**Find $g(4)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\dfrac{x^2-x-12}{x-4}$ is continuous for all real numbers other than $x=4$ which means $g$ is continuous for all real numbers other than $x=4$.

In order for $g$ to also be continuous at $x=4$, the following equality must hold:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4}g(x)=g(4)$

We will obtain the above equality by letting $g(4)=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4}g(x)$.

So let's find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4}g(x)$, come on!

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4}g(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4}\dfrac{x^2-x-12}{x-4}\qquad\gray{\text{This is the rule for }x\neq 4}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4}\dfrac{\cancel{(x-4)}(x+3)}{\cancel{(x-4)}}\qquad\gray{\text{Factor}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4}{(x+3)}{}\qquad\gray{\text{Cancel common factors}}
\\\\
&\small\text{(This is allowed because }x\neq 4)
\\\\
&=4+3\qquad\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=7
\end{align}$

We obtained that if we set $g(4)=7$, then $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4}g(x)=g(4)$, which makes $g$ continuous at $x=4$.

Since we already saw that $g$ is continuous for any other real number, we can determine that it's continuous for all real numbers.
In conclusion, $g(4)=7$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $h(x)=\dfrac{x^2-49}{x+7}$ when $x\neq -7$.

$h$ is continuous for all real numbers.

**Find $h(-7)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\dfrac{x^2-49}{x+7}$ is continuous for all real numbers other than $x=-7$ which means $h$ is continuous for all real numbers other than $x=-7$.

In order for $h$ to also be continuous at $x=-7$, the following equality must hold:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -7}h(x)=h(-7)$

We will obtain the above equality by letting $h(-7)=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -7}h(x)$.

So let's find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -7}h(x)$, come on!

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -7}h(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -7}\dfrac{x^2-49}{x+7}\qquad\gray{\text{This is the rule for }x\neq -7}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -7}\dfrac{\cancel{(x+7)}(x-7)}{\cancel{(x+7)}}\qquad\gray{\text{Factor}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -7}{(x-7)}{}\qquad\gray{\text{Cancel common factors}}
\\\\
&\small\text{(This is allowed because }x\neq -7)
\\\\
&=(-7)-7\qquad\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=-14
\end{align}$

We obtained that if we set $h(-7)=-14$, then $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -7}h(x)=h(-7)$, which makes $h$ continuous at $x=-7$.

Since we already saw that $h$ is continuous for any other real number, we can determine that it's continuous for all real numbers.
In conclusion, $h(-7)=-14$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $f(x)=\dfrac{x+1}{xe^{x}+e^{x}}$ when $x\neq -1$.

$f$ is continuous for all real numbers.

**Find $f(-1)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\dfrac{x+1}{xe^{x}+e^{x}}$ is continuous for all real numbers other than $x=-1$ which means $f$ is continuous for all real numbers other than $x=-1$.

In order for $f$ to also be continuous at $x=-1$, the following equality must hold:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}f(x)=f(-1)$

We will obtain the above equality by letting $f(-1)=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}f(x)$.

So let's find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}f(x)$, come on!

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}f(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}\dfrac{x+1}{xe^{x}+e^{x}}\qquad\gray{\text{This is the rule for }x\neq -1}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}\dfrac{\cancel{(x+1)}}{\cancel{(x+1)}(e^{x})}\qquad\gray{\text{Factor}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}\dfrac{1}{(e^{x})}\qquad\gray{\text{Cancel common factors}}
\\\\
&\small\text{(This is allowed because }x\neq -1)
\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{e^{-1}}\qquad\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=e
\end{align}$

We obtained that if we set $f(-1)=e$, then $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}f(x)=f(-1)$, which makes $f$ continuous at $x=-1$.

Since we already saw that $f$ is continuous for any other real number, we can determine that it's continuous for all real numbers.
In conclusion, $f(-1)=e$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $g(x)=\dfrac{10x}{x^3+5x}$ when $x\neq 0$.

$g$ is continuous for all real numbers.

**Find $g(0)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\dfrac{10x}{x^3+5x}$ is continuous for all real numbers other than $x=0$ which means $g$ is continuous for all real numbers other than $x=0$.

In order for $g$ to also be continuous at $x=0$, the following equality must hold:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}g(x)=g(0)$

We will obtain the above equality by letting $g(0)=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}g(x)$.

So let's find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}g(x)$, come on!

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}g(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\dfrac{10x}{x^3+5x}\qquad\gray{\text{This is the rule for }x\neq 0}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\dfrac{10\cancel{(x)}}{\cancel{(x)}(x^2+5)}\qquad\gray{\text{Factor}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\dfrac{10}{(x^2+5)}\qquad\gray{\text{Cancel common factors}}
\\\\
&\small\text{(This is allowed because }x\neq 0)
\\\\
&=\dfrac{10}{(0)^2+5}\qquad\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=2
\end{align}$

We obtained that if we set $g(0)=2$, then $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}g(x)=g(0)$, which makes $g$ continuous at $x=0$.

Since we already saw that $g$ is continuous for any other real number, we can determine that it's continuous for all real numbers.
In conclusion, $g(0)=2$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $f(x)=\begin{cases}
\dfrac{x+1}{\sqrt{x+5}-2}&\text{for }x\geq-5, x\neq -1
\\\\
k&\text{for }x=-1
\end{cases}$

$f$ is continuous for all $x>-5$.

**What is the value of $k$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\dfrac{x+1}{\sqrt{x+5}-2}$ is continuous for all $x>-5$ other than $x=-1$, which means $f$ is continuous for all $x>-5$ other than $x=-1$.

In order for $f$ to also be continuous at $x=-1$, the following equality must hold:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}f(x)=f(-1)$

Since $f(-1)=k$, we will obtain the above equality by letting $k=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}f(x)$.

So let's find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}f(x)$, come on!

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}f(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}\dfrac{x+1}{\sqrt{x+5}-2}\qquad\gray{\text{This is the rule for }x\neq-1}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}\dfrac{x+1}{\sqrt{x+5}-2}\cdot\dfrac{\sqrt{x+5}+2}{\sqrt{x+5}+2}\qquad\gray{\text{Rationalize}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}\dfrac{(x+1)(\sqrt{x+5}+2)}{(x+5)-(2)^2}\qquad\gray{\text{Simplify}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}\dfrac{\cancel{(x+1)}(\sqrt{x+5}+2)}{\cancel{x+1}}\qquad\gray{\text{Cancel common factors}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}(\sqrt{x+5}+2)
\\\\
&\small\text{(This is allowed because }x\neq -1)
\\\\
&=\sqrt{(-1)+5}+2\qquad\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=4
\end{align}$

We obtained that if we set $k=4$, then $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}f(x)=f(-1)$, which makes $f$ continuous at $x=-1$.

Since we already saw that $f$ is continuous for any other $x>-5$, we can determine that it's continuous for all $x>-5$.
In conclusion, $k=4$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $g(x)=\begin{cases}
\dfrac{x-3}{\sqrt{x+13}-4}&\text{for }x\geq -13, x\neq 3
\\\\
k&\text{for }x=3
\end{cases}$

$g$ is continuous for all $x>-13$.

**What is the value of $k$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\dfrac{x-3}{\sqrt{x+13}-4}$ is continuous for all $x>-13$ other than $x=3$, which means $g$ is continuous for all $x>-13$ other than $x=3$.

In order for $g$ to also be continuous at $x=3$, the following equality must hold:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}g(x)=g(3)$

Since $g(3)=k$, we will obtain the above equality by letting $k=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}g(x)$.

So let's find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}g(x)$, come on!

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}g(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}\dfrac{x-3}{\sqrt{x+13}-4}\qquad\gray{\text{This is the rule for }x\neq 3}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}\dfrac{x-3}{\sqrt{x+13}-4}\cdot\dfrac{\sqrt{x+13}+4}{\sqrt{x+13}+4}\qquad\gray{\text{Rationalize}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}\dfrac{(x-3)(\sqrt{x+13}+4)}{x+13-4^2}\qquad\gray{\text{Simplify}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}\dfrac{\cancel{(x-3)}(\sqrt{x+13}+4)}{\cancel{x-3}}\qquad\gray{\text{Cancel common factors}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}(\sqrt{x+13}+4)
\\\\
&\small\text{(This is allowed because }x\neq 3)
\\\\
&=\sqrt{3+13}+4\qquad\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=8
\end{align}$

We obtained that if we set $k=8$, then $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}g(x)=g(3)$, which makes $g$ continuous at $x=3$.

Since we already saw that $g$ is continuous for any other $x>-13$, we can determine that it's continuous for all $x>-13$.
In conclusion, $k=8$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $h(x)=\begin{cases}
\dfrac{\sqrt{x+26}-5}{x+1}&\text{for }x\geq -26, x\neq -1
\\\\
k&\text{for }x=-1
\end{cases}$

$h$ is continuous for all $x>-26$.

**What is the value of $k$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\dfrac{\sqrt{x+26}-5}{x+1}$ is continuous for all $x>-26$ other than $x=-1$, which means $h$ is continuous for all $x>-26$ other than $x=-1$.

In order for $h$ to also be continuous at $x=-1$, the following equality must hold:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}h(x)=h(-1)$

Since $h(-1)=k$, we will obtain the above equality by letting $k=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}h(x)$.

So let's find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}h(x)$, come on!
In conclusion, $k=\dfrac{1}{10}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $g(x)=\begin{cases}
\dfrac{\sqrt{x+20}-4}{x+4}&\text{for }x\geq -20, x\neq -4
\\\\
k&\text{for }x=-4
\end{cases}$

$g$ is continuous for all $x>-20$.

**What is the value of $k$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\dfrac{\sqrt{x+20}-4}{x+4}$ is continuous for all $x>-20$ other than $x=-4$, which means $g$ is continuous for all $x>-20$ other than $x=-4$.

In order for $g$ to also be continuous at $x=-4$, the following equality must hold:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4}g(x)=g(-4)$

Since $g(-4)=k$, we will obtain the above equality by letting $k=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4}g(x)$.

So let's find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4}g(x)$, come on!
In conclusion, $k=\dfrac{1}{8}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $h(x)=\dfrac{x-2}{\sqrt{x+7}-3}$ when $x\neq 2$.

$h$ is continuous for all $x>-7$.

**Find $h(2)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\dfrac{x-2}{\sqrt{x+7}-3}$ is continuous for all $x>-7$ other than $x=2$, which means $h$ is continuous for all $x>-7$ other than $x=2$.

In order for $h$ to also be continuous at $x=2$, the following equality must hold:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2}h(x)=h(2)$

We will obtain the above equality by letting $h(2)=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2}h(x)$.

So let's find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2}h(x)$, come on!

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2}h(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2}\dfrac{x-2}{\sqrt{x+7}-3}\qquad\gray{\text{This is the rule for }x\neq 2}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2}\dfrac{x-2}{\sqrt{x+7}-3}\cdot\dfrac{\sqrt{x+7}+3}{\sqrt{x+7}+3}\qquad\gray{\text{Rationalize}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2}\dfrac{(x-2)(\sqrt{x+7}+3)}{x+7-3^2}\qquad\gray{\text{Simplify}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2}\dfrac{\cancel{(x-2)}(\sqrt{x+7}+3)}{\cancel{x-2}}\qquad\gray{\text{Cancel common factors}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2}(\sqrt{x+7}+3)
\\\\
&\small\text{(This is allowed because }x\neq 2)
\\\\
&=\sqrt{2+7}+3\qquad\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=6
\end{align}$

We obtained that if we set $h(2)=6$, then $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2}h(x)=h(2)$, which makes $h$ continuous at $x=2$.

Since we already saw that $h$ is continuous for any other $x>-7$, we can determine that it's continuous for all $x>-7$.
In conclusion, $k=6$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $g(x)=\dfrac{x-5}{\sqrt{x-4}-1}$ when $x\neq 5$.

$g$ is continuous for all $x>4$.

**Find $g(5)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\dfrac{x-5}{\sqrt{x-4}-1}$ is continuous for all $x>4$ other than $x=5$, which means $g$ is continuous for all $x>4$ other than $x=5$.

In order for $g$ to also be continuous at $x=5$, the following equality must hold:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 5}g(x)=g(5)$

We will obtain the above equality by letting $g(5)=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 5}g(x)$.

So let's find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 5}g(x)$, come on!

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 5}g(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 5}\dfrac{x-5}{\sqrt{x-4}-1}\qquad\gray{\text{This is the rule for }x\neq 5}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 5}\dfrac{x-5}{\sqrt{x-4}-1}\cdot\dfrac{\sqrt{x-4}+1}{\sqrt{x-4}+1}\qquad\gray{\text{Rationalize}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 5}\dfrac{(x-5)(\sqrt{x-4}+1)}{x-4-1^2}\qquad\gray{\text{Simplify}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 5}\dfrac{\cancel{(x-5)}(\sqrt{x-4}+1)}{\cancel{x-5}}\qquad\gray{\text{Cancel common factors}}
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 5}(\sqrt{x-4}+1)
\\\\
&\small\text{(This is allowed because }x\neq 5)
\\\\
&=\sqrt{5-4}+1\qquad\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=2
\end{align}$

We obtained that if we set $g(5)=2$, then $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 5}g(x)=g(5)$, which makes $g$ continuous at $x=5$.

Since we already saw that $g$ is continuous for any other $x>4$, we can determine that it's continuous for all $x>4$.
In conclusion, $k=2$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $f(x)=\dfrac{\sqrt{x-1}-2}{x-5}$ when $x\neq 5$.

$f$ is continuous for all $x>1$.

**Find $f(5)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\dfrac{\sqrt{x-1}-2}{x-5}$ is continuous for all $x>1$ other than $x=5$, which means $f$ is continuous for all $x>1$ other than $x=5$.

In order for $f$ to also be continuous at $x=5$, the following equality must hold:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 5}f(x)=f(5)$

We will obtain the above equality by letting $f(5)=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 5}f(x)$.

So let's find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 5}f(x)$, come on!
In conclusion, $k=\dfrac{1}{4}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $h(x)=\dfrac{\sqrt{x+12}-3}{x+3}$ when $x\neq -3$.

$h$ is continuous for all $x>-12$.

**Find $h(-3)$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\dfrac{\sqrt{x+12}-3}{x+3}$ is continuous for all $x>-12$ other than $x=-3$, which means $h$ is continuous for all $x>-12$ other than $x=-3$.

In order for $h$ to also be continuous at $x=-3$, the following equality must hold:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}h(x)=h(-3)$

We will obtain the above equality by letting $h(-3)=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}h(x)$.

So let's find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}h(x)$, come on!
In conclusion, $k=\dfrac{1}{6}$.
